step_by_step_thinking: Frozen section is primarily discussed in the context of confirming parathyroid tissue rather than analyzing thyroid parenchyma or diagnosing malignancy in multinodular goiter. For multinodular thyroid disease, diagnosis relies heavily on fine-needle aspiration (FNA) and subsequent grading using Bethesda criteria; definitive differentiation between benign follicular adenoma and follicular thyroid carcinoma requires pathological confirmation after full thyroidectomy—specifically looking for capsular or vascular invasion—which is only achievable with a complete excision specimen. No evidence suggests that frozen section alters surgical decisions related to subtotal vs. total thyroidectomy in multinodular goiter beyond what would already be guided by FNA and sonographic findings.
raw_response:
Frozen section is primarily discussed in the context of confirming parathyroid tissue rather than analyzing thyroid parenchyma or diagnosing malignancy in multinodular goiter. For multinodular thyroid disease, diagnosis relies heavily on fine-needle aspiration (FNA) and subsequent grading using Bethesda criteria; definitive differentiation between benign follicular adenoma and follicular thyroid carcinoma requires pathological confirmation after full thyroidectomy—specifically looking for capsular or vascular invasion—which is only achievable with a complete excision specimen. No evidence suggests that frozen section alters surgical decisions related to subtotal vs. total thyroidectomy in multinodular goiter beyond what would already be guided by FNA and sonographic findings.
\boxed{B}
